name: Lugones, Lugo de Llanera and Noreña Circular
name_original: Lugones, Lugo de Llanera y Noreña — ruta circular
description: This moderate 21.5-mile touring cycling route explores the scenic Asturian landscape around Lugones, Lugo de Llanera, and Noreña.
region_intro: Hiking around Siero, a municipality in central Asturias, offers diverse landscapes for outdoor activities. The region is characterized by green vistas, river valleys, and surrounding mountains, providing varied terrain for exploration. Siero's strategic position within Asturias also grants access to broader natural attractions, including protected areas and the Cantabrian Mountains. Numerous local trails, known as "sendas," wind through its natural spaces, catering to different hiking preferences. Best hiking trails around Siero The most popular hiking route is River trail of the Nora River from Pola de Siero, a 9.1 miles (14.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 46 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows a scenic riverside path. Another top favourite among local hikers is Camino del Norte de El Berrón a Pola de Siero — circular por la Senda Fluvial del Río Nora, a moderate 8.2 miles (13.2 km) path. This trail combines a section of the historic Camino del Norte with the tranquil Nora River path. Local hikers also love the Nora River Greenway loop from La Pola Siero, an 8.1 miles (13.0 km) trail leading through riverside green spaces, often completed in about 3 hours 24 minutes. Hiking around Siero is defined by its river paths, greenways, and access to varied Asturian landscapes. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and challenging routes. The routes in Siero are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 400 hikers have used komoot to explore Siero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Siero?
A: Siero offers a diverse network of over 50 hiking trails. These routes cater to various skill levels, including easy, moderate, and a few more challenging options, ensuring there's something for every hiker.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Siero?
A: Hiking in Siero will immerse you in lush green vistas, tranquil river valleys, and rolling hills. The municipality's central location in Asturias also provides access to broader natural attractions, including glimpses of the Cantabrian Mountains and the region's renowned natural parks.

Q: Are there easy trails suitable for beginners or families in Siero?
A: Yes, Siero has 15 easy trails perfect for beginners or families. Many of these are 'sendas verdes' or greenways, which often utilize old railway lines, providing accessible and relatively flat paths. A good example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14991492" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Nora Riverside Trail – Nora Riverside Trail loop from Pola de Siero / La Pola Siero&lt;/a&gt;, which is an easy 7.6 km route.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Siero?
A: Absolutely. Many trails in Siero are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048571576/camino-del-norte-de-el-berron-a-pola-de-siero-circular-por-la-senda-fluvial-del-rio-nora" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Norte de El Berrón a Pola de Siero — circular por la Senda Fluvial del Río Nora&lt;/a&gt;, which combines a historic path with a scenic riverside section.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Siero?
A: Many trails in Siero are dog-friendly, especially the numerous 'sendas' and riverside paths. It's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas where livestock might be present or near protected natural spaces, and to clean up after them to preserve the natural environment for everyone.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can visit near the hiking trails?
A: Siero's strategic location means you can easily access several interesting landmarks. You might consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/210761" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sacred Heart of Jesus Monument on Monte Naranco&lt;/a&gt;, which offers panoramic views. Other nearby points of interest include various mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5619300" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Santo Emiliano&lt;/a&gt; and summits such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5599992" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Les Praeres – Sierra de Peñamayor&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there trails with good viewpoints or natural features in Siero?
A: Yes, Siero and its surroundings offer trails with rewarding views. Routes leading to elevated points like Picu Fariu or those providing access to Monte Naranco offer stunning panoramic vistas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31894906" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Pedrín Cave – View of Picos de Europa loop from Vega&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that provides views towards the Picos de Europa.

Q: How can I get to the trailheads in Siero using public transport?
A: Siero is centrally located in Asturias, making many areas accessible by public transport. Key towns like Pola de Siero often serve as hubs with bus connections to surrounding villages and trailheads. It's advisable to check local bus schedules for specific routes and starting points.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads in Siero?
A: Parking is generally available near popular trailheads and in the towns and villages that serve as starting points for hikes in Siero. While specific designated parking areas vary, you can often find roadside parking or small lots, especially in areas like Pola de Siero or near the 'sendas'.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Siero?
A: Siero, like much of Asturias, is beautiful year-round. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant natural colors, making them ideal for hiking. Summer is also popular, though it can be warmer. Even winter hiking is possible, with milder temperatures than many other regions, though some higher elevation trails might require more preparation.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Siero?
A: The hiking trails in Siero are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 30 reviews. Hikers often praise the region's diverse landscapes, the tranquility of the riverside paths, and the accessibility of routes that cater to various fitness levels.

Q: Are there any riverside walks in Siero?
A: Riverside walks are a defining feature of hiking in Siero. The Senda Fluvial del Río Nora is a prominent example, offering scenic paths along the Nora River. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048575820/river-trail-of-the-nora-river-from-pola-de-siero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;River trail of the Nora River from Pola de Siero&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate route that follows this beautiful fluvial path.
tips: Lugones is a small town known for its industrial heritage, as it was once a major center of coal mining and steel production. One of the main landmarks of Lugones is the Church of San Félix, a 12th century Romanesque temple declared a National Monument. The city also boasts several other notable buildings, such as the Palacio de Villabona, a 16th-century mansion now used as a cultural center.

Lugo de Llanera is a small town known for its beautiful natural surroundings and traditional Asturian architecture. Some of the notable landmarks include the Iglesia de San Miguel, which dates back to the 17th century and features an impressive Baroque altar, and the Palacio de Valdecarzana, a 16th-century Renaissance building that now serves as the town hall. The village is surrounded by beautiful countryside, with rolling hills and lush forests, and the River Nora is a popular spot for fishing and picnicking.

Noreña is a small town known for its beautiful landscapes, rich history, and traditional cuisine. One of the main landmarks of Noreña is the Church of San Juan, a 13th century Romanesque temple declared a National Monument. The city also boasts several other notable buildings, such as the Palacio de Camposagrado, a 17th-century mansion that now serves as the town hall. Noreña is famous for its gastronomy, especially for its meat dishes, such as the famous Asturian T-bone steak, which is made with beef from the area.

faqs.question: How difficult is this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as a moderate touring bicycle ride. While generally considered practically flat, especially along sections accompanying the Nora River, it does include short, steep climbs, such as the ascent from the Roman bridge to the Santa Eulalia church in Colloto. It's suitable for cyclists with good fitness.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: You can expect mostly paved surfaces, which are ideal for touring bicycles. However, some segments may be unpaved and could present a challenge. The route often follows less-trafficked local roads and green paths, offering a serene cycling experience.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 34.7 km (21.6 miles) long and typically takes around 2 hours and 12 minutes to complete by touring bicycle, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The trail showcases the lush greenery of Asturias, suggesting that spring and early autumn would be ideal for pleasant weather and vibrant scenery. Avoid periods of heavy rain if possible, as some unpaved sections might become muddy.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse scenery and cultural highlights. You'll pass by the Church of San Félix de Lugones, the Church of San Miguel de Lugo de Llanera, and the Plaza Mayor of Noreña. You can also enjoy tranquil stretches alongside the Nora River, including the medieval Puente Romanón (or Puente Viejo), and the recreational area of Santa Bárbara near Lugones.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this route?
faqs.answer: As a circular route starting and ending in Lugones, you can typically find parking within Lugones itself to begin your ride. Specific parking areas are not detailed, but public parking options should be available in the town.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The trail research does not explicitly state whether dogs are allowed on this specific route. Generally, on public roads and paths in Asturias, dogs are permitted but should be kept on a leash, especially in populated areas or near livestock.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths through the Asturian landscape.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths for short sections, including the Ruta Xixon - Puertu Payares, Vía de la Plata, La Belga - Viella - Parque Periurbano de Les Folgueres, and Senda de La Acebera, among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several towns, including Lugones, Lugo de Llanera, and Noreña. These towns offer opportunities to stop for refreshments, cafes, and other amenities.

faqs.question: What should I know about navigating the route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily uses less-trafficked local roads and green paths, offering a peaceful experience. However, be aware that the final section entering Lugones may have more vehicle movement. While generally flat, be prepared for short, steep climbs in certain areas.
